subject: history
prompt: In what year did William of Normandy invade England?
explainer: 1066 — the Battle of Hastings. Often used as the date that marks the end of Anglo-Saxon England.

subject: history
prompt: Which battle in 1066 made William the new king of England?
explainer: Battle of Hastings, 14 October 1066. Harold Godwinson was killed; William became known as William the Conqueror.

subject: history
prompt: What was the Domesday Book?
options: A survey of English land and wealth
explainer: Completed in 1086, the Domesday Book recorded who owned what land and how much it was worth — so William could tax it.

subject: history
prompt: Which Anglo-Saxon king was defeated and killed at the Battle of Hastings?
explainer: Harold Godwinson was crowned in early 1066 and died at Hastings in October that year — possibly shot in the eye, as the Bayeux Tapestry suggests.

subject: history
prompt: Just before Hastings, Harold marched north to defeat which Viking king at Stamford Bridge?
explainer: Harold beat Harald Hardrada at Stamford Bridge in September 1066, then had to race south to face William — exhausting his army before Hastings.

subject: history
prompt: After winning, William used which type of fortification to control England?
explainer: Motte (mound) and bailey (enclosed yard) castles were quick to build with timber and earth. Stone keeps came later as Norman power was secured.

subject: history
prompt: What does the Bayeux Tapestry mainly depict?
options: The Norman Conquest and Battle of Hastings
explainer: A 70-metre embroidered cloth telling the Norman version of the events leading to William's victory in 1066.

subject: history
prompt: The "Harrying of the North" (1069–70) was William's response to:
explainer: William's troops burned villages, crops and livestock across northern England to crush rebellion — thousands died of starvation that winter.

subject: history
prompt: Which language did the Normans bring to England, heavily influencing modern English?
explainer: Norman French became the language of the court and law for centuries. That's why English has so many French-origin words (e.g. "beef", "justice").

subject: history
prompt: In the feudal system, who was at the top?
explainer: King → Barons → Knights → Peasants. The King granted land in return for loyalty and military service.

subject: history
prompt: What was Magna Carta (1215)?
options: A charter limiting the king's power
explainer: Barons forced King John to sign Magna Carta, which set limits on what the king could do — early step toward the rule of law.

subject: history
prompt: Below the king in the feudal system, who held land directly from him?
explainer: Barons (also called tenants-in-chief) held large estates from the king and supplied him with knights and taxes.

subject: history
prompt: What was a serf in medieval England?
options: A peasant tied to a lord's land
explainer: Serfs were unfree peasants who could not leave the manor without their lord's permission. They worked the land in return for protection.

subject: history
prompt: What was a manor in medieval England?
options: A lord's estate worked by peasants
explainer: The manor was the basic economic unit — a lord's house, fields, woods and village, run by peasants who owed labour and rent.

subject: history
prompt: Knights were expected to follow a code of behaviour known as:
explainer: Chivalry covered honour, courage, fair play in battle, and protection of the weak — the medieval ideal of how a knight should behave.

subject: history
prompt: What were the Crusades?
options: Christian military expeditions to retake the Holy Land
explainer: From the late 11th century, Christian armies from Europe fought to take Jerusalem and the Holy Land from Muslim rule.

subject: history
prompt: Alongside the king, which institution held huge power in medieval England?
explainer: The Catholic Church owned land, ran schools, controlled marriage and burial, and could even excommunicate kings.

subject: history
prompt: Thomas Becket, murdered in 1170, was:
explainer: Becket was killed in Canterbury Cathedral after clashing with King Henry II over the Church's independence. He was later made a saint.

subject: history
prompt: The Peasants' Revolt of 1381 was triggered partly by:
explainer: An unpopular poll tax (the same amount for everyone) plus post-plague resentment led peasants — under Wat Tyler — to march on London.

subject: history
prompt: When did the Black Death first reach England?
explainer: Arrived in 1348. It killed perhaps a third of the English population within a few years.

subject: history
prompt: What kind of disease was the Black Death (mostly)?
options: A plague spread by fleas on rats
explainer: Bubonic plague, mainly spread by fleas living on black rats that travelled with traders.

subject: history
prompt: After the Black Death, peasant wages tended to:
explainer: So many people had died that workers could demand more pay. This shifted power away from landowners.

subject: history
prompt: Roughly what fraction of England's population is thought to have died in the Black Death?
explainer: Estimates vary, but around 30–40% of the population died between 1348 and 1350 — a catastrophic loss.

subject: history
prompt: A famous symptom of the bubonic plague was:
options: A bright red rash on the legs only
explainer: Buboes — swollen, blackened lymph nodes in the neck, armpits or groin — gave the Black Death its name.

subject: history
prompt: Who was the first Tudor monarch?
explainer: Henry VII (Henry Tudor) won the Battle of Bosworth in 1485 and started the Tudor line.

subject: history
prompt: How many wives did Henry VIII have?
explainer: Six wives. Mnemonic for their fates: divorced, beheaded, died; divorced, beheaded, survived.

subject: history
prompt: Why did Henry VIII break with the Catholic Church?
options: He wanted a divorce the Pope wouldn't grant
explainer: The Pope refused to annul Henry's marriage to Catherine of Aragon, so Henry made himself head of the new Church of England.

subject: history
prompt: Elizabeth I's reign is sometimes called the "Golden Age" because of:
options: Defeating the Spanish Armada and a flourishing of arts (e.g. Shakespeare)
explainer: 1588 Armada victory plus Shakespeare and exploration made her reign a high point of English culture and power.

subject: history
prompt: Which Tudor queen was nicknamed "Bloody Mary"?
explainer: Mary I had hundreds of Protestants burned at the stake while trying to restore Catholicism — earning the nickname "Bloody Mary".

subject: history
prompt: Henry VII won which battle in 1485 to start the Tudor dynasty?
explainer: At Bosworth, Henry Tudor defeated Richard III — ending the Wars of the Roses and the Plantagenet dynasty.

subject: history
prompt: The Wars of the Roses (1455–87) were fought between which two royal houses?
explainer: The white rose of York fought the red rose of Lancaster. Henry Tudor (a Lancastrian) ended the wars and married Elizabeth of York to unite them.

subject: history
prompt: Edward VI, Henry VIII's only son, is best known for:
explainer: Edward was crowned aged 9 and ruled briefly. Under him, England moved further from Catholicism — but he died at 15.

subject: history
prompt: In what year did the Gunpowder Plot try to blow up Parliament?
explainer: 5 November 1605. We still mark the date with bonfires and fireworks every year.

subject: history
prompt: Which king were the gunpowder plotters trying to kill?
explainer: James I — the first Stuart king of England. The Catholic plotters hoped a new monarch would treat them better.

subject: history
prompt: Which plotter was caught guarding the barrels of gunpowder?
explainer: Guy (Guido) Fawkes was discovered in the cellars beneath Parliament with 36 barrels of gunpowder. Catesby was the ringleader.

subject: history
prompt: The main reason for the Gunpowder Plot was:
options: Anger at the treatment of Catholics in England
explainer: Catholics had hoped James I would relax anti-Catholic laws. When he didn't, plotters wanted to kill him and his Protestant Parliament.

subject: history
prompt: In which years did the English Civil War take place?
explainer: Parliament and the King fought on and off from 1642 to 1651. Charles I was executed in 1649.

subject: history
prompt: The two sides in the English Civil War were:
explainer: Cavaliers backed King Charles I; Roundheads (named after their short hair) backed Parliament. Religion and money were both factors.

subject: history
prompt: A key cause of the English Civil War was:
options: The king clashing with Parliament over money and power
explainer: Charles I tried to rule without Parliament, raised taxes himself, and clashed over religion. Parliament refused to be sidelined.

subject: history
prompt: Which decisive 1645 battle did Parliament's New Model Army win?
explainer: At Naseby, the disciplined New Model Army (with Cromwell in command of cavalry) crushed the Royalist forces.

subject: history
prompt: Charles I was put on trial and executed in:
explainer: January 1649 — Charles I was beheaded outside the Banqueting House in Whitehall after being found guilty of treason against his own people.

subject: history
prompt: After the king's execution, Oliver Cromwell ruled England as:
explainer: From 1653 Cromwell ruled as "Lord Protector" of the Commonwealth — England's only experiment with not having a monarch.

subject: history
prompt: Life under Cromwell's rule was famously:
options: Very relaxed — pubs open all day
Strict and Puritan — many entertainments were banned
explainer: Theatres were shut, Christmas celebrations banned, and Sunday strictly observed. Many ordinary people resented the harsh Puritan laws.

subject: history
prompt: What happened to the monarchy after Cromwell died in 1658?
options: Charles II was invited back — the "Restoration" of 1660
explainer: Cromwell's son Richard was a weak ruler. In 1660, Parliament invited Charles I's son Charles II back from exile — the Restoration.

subject: history
prompt: Oliver Cromwell ruled England as:
explainer: After the king was executed (1649) and the monarchy abolished, Cromwell took the title 'Lord Protector' — effectively a dictator, ruling 1653–1658.

subject: history
prompt: What happened to the monarchy soon after Cromwell's death?
options: Charles II was invited back (the Restoration, 1660)
explainer: Cromwell died in 1658. His son could not hold power. By 1660, Parliament invited Charles II to take the throne — the 'Restoration'.

subject: history
prompt: Who is the most famous plotter, caught guarding the gunpowder?
explainer: Guy Fawkes was caught in the cellars beneath Parliament on the night of 4 November 1605. His name lives on in Bonfire Night.
